critic_raw:
This multi-modal deep learning vs. logistic regression readmission prediction design has a critical split flaw that the authors themselves acknowledge, and a test set contamination problem.

The design itself flags a fundamental problem: 'This approach aims to maintain class balance but may unintentionally mix future and past data in training and test sets.' This is not a minor caveat — it is a statement that the split design is known to violate temporal integrity. For a clinical prediction task where readmission risk changes with evolving treatment protocols, drug availability, population demographics, and seasonal effects, a random split that mixes 2019 patients into the test set alongside 2024 patients means the model is evaluated on data that may predate its training examples. This directly inflates performance by allowing the model to train on later, harder-to-predict cases and test on earlier, easier cases.

The model selection process includes a test set peek. Section 6 states: 'a final check is run on the test set to verify that the selected configuration maintains its advantage before committing to full training on the combined train+validation set.' This is explicit test set contamination. The selected hyperparameter configuration was chosen by validation performance, then verified on the test set before committing — this is multiple testing on the test set. If the first selected configuration had failed this 'final check,' a different configuration would presumably have been selected. The test set is no longer held-out; it has been used as a secondary selection criterion.

The genomic features (rare variant burden, polygenic risk scores, copy-number variations) are described as predictors of 30-day readmission. This is a strong distributional assumption that requires justification. Germline genetics do not change between admission and readmission; they may correlate with disease severity and comorbidities that do predict readmission, but the mechanism is indirect and the effect size is likely small relative to clinical features. Including genomic features without validating their marginal contribution (e.g., via an ablation comparing EHR-only model to EHR+genomic model) means the multi-modal model has more features but no evidence that the added modality is informative for this specific outcome.

The baseline is logistic regression on EHR features only (no clinical notes, no genomic data), while the proposed model includes EHR, clinical notes, and genomic data. The comparison tests multi-modal integration + model architecture simultaneously, not multi-modal integration alone. If the proposed model outperforms the baseline, it is impossible to attribute the improvement to genomics, clinical notes, or architecture. Adding an EHR-only deep learning baseline would be the minimal control needed to separate these effects.

TF-IDF vectorization for clinical notes uses 'vocabulary fit on training notes only' — this is correctly done. However, the frozen pre-trained clinical BERT embeddings do not have this problem since they were pre-trained on external data. The design does not specify whether the pre-trained BERT model is fine-tuned on the training set or used purely as a feature extractor. If fine-tuned on training data, the fine-tuning procedure, early stopping criterion, and whether the BERT component is updated end-to-end are unspecified.
all_issues_raised: The design explicitly acknowledges that the random split may mix future and past data across train and test sets — this known temporal integrity violation means test performance can be inflated by evaluating the model on earlier cases after training on later cases.

The test set is used for a 'final check' to verify configuration advantage before committing to full training — this is multiple testing on the held-out set and violates the holdout protocol, making test performance estimates optimistically biased.

The multi-modal model adds genomic features without an ablation showing marginal contribution of genomics to 30-day readmission prediction — the outcome may not be genetically predictable at the relevant time scale, and the added modality may add noise rather than signal.

The comparison tests multi-modal integration and model architecture simultaneously; without an EHR-only deep learning baseline, improvements cannot be attributed to the additional data modalities versus the neural architecture.

The use of pre-trained clinical BERT embeddings is underspecified — whether the BERT component is fine-tuned on training data or used as a frozen feature extractor is not stated, leaving a critical modeling decision uncontrolled.
all_issues_adjudicated: The design explicitly acknowledges that the random split may mix future and past data across train and test sets — this known temporal integrity violation means test performance can be inflated by evaluating the model on earlier cases after training on later cases.

The test set is used for a 'final check' to verify configuration advantage before committing to full training — this is multiple testing on the held-out set and violates the holdout protocol, making test performance estimates optimistically biased.

The multi-modal model adds genomic features without an ablation showing marginal contribution of genomics to 30-day readmission prediction — the outcome may not be genetically predictable at the relevant time scale, and the added modality may add noise rather than signal.

The comparison tests multi-modal integration and model architecture simultaneously; without an EHR-only deep learning baseline, improvements cannot be attributed to the additional data modalities versus the neural architecture.

The use of pre-trained clinical BERT embeddings is underspecified — whether the BERT component is fine-tuned on training data or used as a frozen feature extractor is not stated, leaving a critical modeling decision uncontrolled.
